narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 34, you've put together a respectable profile for law school, including your middle-income family background and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 57.6%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction stems from a careful look at key indicators from past students' data, focusing on how features like your early and later school grades stack up. The most influential factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, which strongly signaled higher bar exam risk compared to typical students averaging around the middle of their class. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which, while decent, pulled in the opposite direction by suggesting a better chance of passing than the model might expect from lower scorers. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed toward the failure prediction, as starting in the bottom tenth set a concerning pattern early on, unlike the average student's mid-range performance. These three academic markers—your low deciles in Years 1 and 3 outweighing the positive lift from your LSAT—were the primary drivers tipping the scales against you, even with your GPA close to the 3.21 average. In comparison to typical admitted students, who often show stronger decile rankings throughout school, your profile highlighted vulnerabilities in consistent performance that the model associates with bar struggles. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above the 36.7 average, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or explanations in a reapplications essay, and gathering strong recommendation letters emphasizing your full-time commitment. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at schools that value growth stories like yours. With persistence, you're well-positioned to rewrite this outcome.
